project.name: sandcastle-incus
project.description: Sandcastle Incus is the Incus-backed implementation of Sandcastle v1: a Go CLI (sandcastle/sc) plus Auth App that provisions multi-tenant, tenant-scoped Incus container machines for AI and development workflows, wires them onto a user-selected Tailscale network, serves private tenant DNS, and publishes HTTP routes through shared-infrastructure Caddy.

tour.title: Project Overview & Domain Vocabulary
tour.description: Start here to learn what Sandcastle Incus is: a Go CLI plus Auth App that provisions multi-tenant infrastructure on Incus, connects tenant machines to Tailscale, serves private DNS, and publishes HTTP routes through Caddy. The README gives the product framing and build story, while CONTEXT.md and the glossary define the canonical domain nouns — tenant, project, machine, route, sidecar, workload identity — that every file in the codebase uses by name. Read these first so the code that follows reads in the project's own vocabulary.

tour.title: The Fat-Binary Entry Point
tour.description: Everything runs from one binary built at cmd/sandcastle. This busybox-style entry point inspects argv[0] and dispatches to a different role depending on the invocation name: `sc`/`sandcastle` becomes the user CLI, while `sc-adm`/`sandcastle-admin` becomes the admin CLI that also hosts the long-running services. Because the same executable is copied into every appliance the admin deploys, understanding this dispatch is the key to understanding how one file serves the CLI, the Auth App, and the brokers alike.
tour.languageLesson: Go's os.Args[0] carries the name the process was invoked as. Symlinking one binary under several names and branching on argv[0] is the classic busybox multi-call pattern — one build artifact, many roles, no per-tool duplication.

tour.title: The Cobra Command Trees & Dependency Injection
tour.description: Building on the dispatch from Step 2, both command trees are assembled here in internal/cli. root.go constructs the user-facing Cobra tree and the large commandConfig struct — a dependency-injection container holding interface values for every domain capability — while admin_root.go builds the parallel admin tree and wires the service subcommands. create_v2.go is a representative leaf command showing how a user action is turned into a domain plan. This is the seam where user intent meets the domain packages you will explore next.
tour.languageLesson: The commandConfig struct is hand-rolled dependency injection: instead of a DI framework, Go code passes interface fields into each Cobra command, so tests can swap real Incus implementations for fakes just by assigning different struct fields.

tour.title: CLI Device Login
tour.description: The first thing a user does is log in. login.go implements the browser-assisted CLI Device Login: it kicks off GitHub OAuth, polls the Auth App for approval, and on success enrolls the tenant's Incus remote over the tailnet. It talks to the Auth App through the DeviceClient in authapp/client.go, and the server side of the handshake — start, poll, approve, and issue an Incus certificate add token — lives in authapp/device.go. This step bridges the CLI from Step 3 into the Auth App service that owns identity.

tour.title: The Auth App Service
tour.description: The Auth App is a standalone HTTPS service that owns identity and first-login provisioning. app.go bootstraps the server, runs SQLite migrations, and wires all HTTP handlers; github.go abstracts the GitHub OAuth exchange and username verification; store.go is the SQLite-backed user, session, allowlist, and SSH-key store that replaced the old JSON user DB. When a user logs in for the first time, provision.go automatically creates their personal tenant by driving the v2 create plan you will meet in Step 7.
tour.languageLesson: The Auth Database is SQLite, embedded in-process. app.go runs schema migrations at startup, so a single binary carries both the service and its database evolution — no separate migration tool or external DB server to operate.

tour.title: OIDC Provider & Workload Identity
tour.description: Beyond human login, the Auth App is also an OIDC provider for machines. oidc.go exposes per-tenant discovery and JWKS endpoints and manages encrypted signing keys, while workload.go mints short-lived Workload Identity Tokens, authenticating a machine by its Machine Runtime Secret and signing a JWT. On the machine side, machine/workload_identity.go builds the in-guest token-helper files that exchange those tokens for GCP external-account credentials. Together these let a tenant machine prove who it is to cloud providers without long-lived secrets.

tour.title: Tenant Lifecycle
tour.description: This is the heart of the domain — the tenant package has the highest fan-in in the whole codebase, meaning almost everything depends on it. create_plan_v2.go builds the v2 tenant creation plan (cloud-init user data, CA material, DNS, and CIDR allocation), create_plan.go defines the request types and sidecar/CA structures, delete_plan.go plans teardown, and list.go reads live Incus state to enumerate tenants and compute allocated CIDRs. Note the plans are pure data structures: the domain decides *what* should exist, and the Incus layer in Step 9 decides *how* to make it so.

tour.title: Machine Lifecycle
tour.description: Within a tenant live machines — Incus containers and VMs. plan.go is the central machine-domain planner that builds a CreatePlan with IP allocation, devices, certificates, and hostnames; lifecycle.go plans validated start/stop/restart actions; connect.go plans an SSH connection by resolving the target and its managed Linux user; and resolve.go implements the cross-project lookup with unique-search semantics that lets a user name a machine without naming its project. Like the tenant plans in Step 7, these produce plans that the Incus layer executes.

tour.title: The Incus Integration Layer
tour.description: The incusx package is where every abstract plan from Steps 7 and 8 becomes real infrastructure against the live Incus API. tenant_create_v2.go ensures the appliance bridge, project, volumes, profiles, and sidecar; machine_create_v2.go creates instances and drives their lifecycle actions; machine_lifecycle.go applies start/stop/restart plans and heals machine networking afterward. topology.go closes the loop by reading live Incus state — networks, volumes, instances — back into the tenant.Topology snapshot that status and list commands report on.
tour.languageLesson: This layer is the concrete side of Go's interface-based design: the tenant and machine packages declare interfaces (Creator, Controller, TopologyStore); incusx supplies the implementations. The domain never imports Incus types directly, which is why the whole system can be unit-tested without a running cluster.

tour.title: Networking: Tailscale, Private DNS & Public Routes
tour.description: A provisioned machine is only useful once it is reachable. tailscale/up.go plans the `tailscale up` bring-up that joins a machine to the tenant's isolated tailnet; localdns/plan.go configures per-tenant resolvers on the client host so private hostnames resolve; dns/apply.go writes the dnsmasq host records derived from the tenant CIDR. For publicly reachable services, route/plan.go plans public routes (including DNS proof-of-ownership), and routebroker/serve.go runs the privileged, mTLS-authorized Route Broker that actually mutates shared route infrastructure on a user's behalf.

tour.title: Generators & Domain Primitives
tour.description: Underneath every layer sit the shared primitives — naming.go has a fan-in of 46 because nearly every package needs its canonical slug and Incus resource-name logic. cidr/allocator.go carves non-overlapping tenant subnets from the shared pool, certs/certs.go generates the Tenant CA and per-machine leaf certificates, caddy/render.go emits the Caddyfile fragments for reverse proxies, and config/sandcastle.go resolves user and admin configuration across flags, env vars, seed file, and defaults. These are the foundational building blocks the higher layers assemble.

tour.title: Images, the sc-edge Appliance & Build/CI
tour.description: Machines boot from Sandcastle images, and public traffic enters through an edge. images/remote.go plans and scripts remote OCI image builds on the Image Builder appliance and publishes them to GHCR. The sc-edge Dockerfile compiles a layer4-enabled Caddy, and sc-edge/Caddyfile is the single source of truth for its SNI-router-plus-ACME configuration. The Makefile builds the one fat binary and its symlinks, and the ci.yml GitHub Actions workflow runs the safe test tiers on every push and pull request — the capstone that keeps the whole system green.
tour.languageLesson: The sc-edge Dockerfile is a multi-stage build: a caddy:2-builder stage runs xcaddy to compile a plugin-enabled binary, then a slim caddy:2 runtime stage copies only that binary and the Caddyfile. Separating build tooling from the runtime keeps the shipped image small and free of the compiler toolchain.

tour.title: Architecture Decisions (ADRs)
tour.description: Finish with the ADRs under docs/adr, which record the load-bearing decisions the code embodies and explain why the system is shaped the way you just toured it. 0001 establishes the tenant-as-Incus-project boundary that Steps 7 and 9 depend on; 0002 explains the Go-plus-SQLite Auth App from Step 5; 0005 motivates the OIDC provider from Step 6; 0007 defines the infrastructure network and CIDR pool behind Steps 10 and 11; and 0008 describes the secret-bearing Infrastructure Seed File that bootstraps a whole deployment. Read these to turn a tour of the code into an understanding of its rationale.
